text: Hi guys, I'm Dr. Andrew Iranihov from Surgical Oasis Institute in Newport Beach, California. I'm a board-certified general surgeon and my main interest is minimally invasive surgery including robotic and laparoscopic operation. And my main interest in my practice is hernia repair. Today I'm going to discuss with you about minimally invasive inguinal hernia repair without mesh. Obviously, inguinal hernia, which is the defect in the groin region, is a very common problem in the United States, and almost around five hundred thousand people go under this type of surgery every year. The majority of this operation is done with the usage in order to reinforce the groin region and repair the hernia. However, there are patients that are candidate for minimally invasive inguinal hernia repair without the mesh.

Obviously, the standard of hernia repair, whether open or minimally invasive surgery, including laparoscopic and robotic operation, is mainly done with the usage of the mesh. However, in my practice, I'm offering a repair of the adrenopranium without the mesh as well. However, not every patient is a candidate for this operation, so the main purpose of this video is to discuss those patients that are potential candidates for adrenopranium without the mesh. So the patients that are coming in, obviously I will go ahead and examine them. And normally if the hernia is small, especially in females or in the male that they do not see or feel the bulge and they just have discomfort, obviously they can be a candidate for in-worm hernia without the mesh. So normally what we do in this type of procedure is we enter the abdomen through the small incision in the umbilicus. And by placing the camera, I am able to see the defect from inside of the abdomen. And I place two small incisions on the upper portion of the abdomen and enter the special instrumentations that are designed to perform these operations with minimally invasive surgery.

The surgeon evaluates the groin hernia defect, preparing to perform an iliopubic tract repair. This technique involves hand sewing to close the small hernia defect by approximating the muscle to the iliopsoas tract, a thick ligament in the lower abdomen, without using mesh.

Having a very, very large hernia defect, the potential of hernia recurrence is very high. So performing this operation without usage of the mesh in a patient who has a very large hernia defect is not advised. However, if the hernia is small, then you'll be able to close that inherently without the mesh. There's still a possibility of hernia recurrence. that hernia recurrence is really correlated with the patient's body habitus, how strong their muscles are, and then also the size of their hernia. So people who are very muscular, they have a lot of muscles, and also they have a small hernia are a good candidate for minimally invasive hernia repair. In the past, people offered open hernia repair without the mesh, like Shoal Dice technique, but I would not recommend that operation because it is still a very invasive operation because it requires cutting through the skin in the groin area, cutting through the soft tissue, opening up part of the muscles and sewing these muscles very tightly next to the nerve which can cause significant discomfort post-operatively as well as even chronic discomfort and pain due to nerve damage. So my main approach to patients with a smaller hernia would be minimally invasive surgery with a laparoscopic or robotic and going from inside and performing what we call the pubic tract repair and closing the muscle from inside and that way you do not damage the abdominal wall by creating bigger incisions between the

The surgeon demonstrates a laparoscopic repair of an inguinal hernia without mesh, known as ileopubic tract repair. The hernia defect is visible, with the iliopubic ligament on the right side of the image. The surgeon sews the iliopubic ligament to the abdominal wall muscle. The peritoneum is opened, revealing the preperitoneal space, and the hernia sac and fat are removed. In this female patient, the round ligament is visible on the left, passing through the hernia defect. The defect is repaired with absorbable sutures, and the peritoneum is closed with absorbable sutures.
